title: Power BI - Scenario And Learning Path Routing Directory
applies_to: Routing Power BI requests by user goal, skill level, and solution shape
Power BI learning and adoption planning
Choosing the right Power BI documentation branch for implementation work

confirmed_facts.fact: The Power BI learning path directory provides curated sequential documentation paths for common Power BI scenarios and is intended to take a user from start to finish for a specific use case.

The scenario selection guide currently identifies fifteen Power BI paths: new user, Excel user, business user, report creator, Copilot user, data analyst, advanced report creator, collaborator, Fabric user, mobile user, admin, developer, DirectQuery user, print-ready reports, and goals and metrics.
The report creator path explicitly includes report authoring, publishing to the service, dashboard creation, refresh, scheduled refresh, and sharing.

The data analyst path emphasizes data sources, Power Query, modeling view, relationships, measures, calculated columns, DAX, time intelligence, calculation groups, tabular modeling, and an end-to-end dimensional model tutorial.

The developer path focuses on embedding, Power BI REST API, Fabric REST APIs, Tabular Model Scripting Language, custom visuals, visuals API, AppSource publishing, and row-level security in embedded scenarios.
The DirectQuery path centers on live connections, import versus DirectQuery versus composite models, Direct Lake, Fabric lakehouses, and DirectQuery model guidance.
The print-ready reports path routes users to paginated reports and Power BI Report Builder rather than standard interactive report pages in Power BI Desktop.

scenario_selection_rules: Use the report creator path when the user wants standard interactive report authoring, publishing, dashboards, refresh, and sharing.
Use the data analyst path when the request is primarily about connection, shaping, modeling, DAX, relationships, or time-intelligence design.
Use the advanced report creator path when the user wants bookmarks, drillthrough, custom visuals, conditional formatting, accessibility, or report-performance tuning.
Use the collaborator path when the request is about workspaces, apps, Teams or SharePoint embedding, subscriptions, endorsement, or deployment pipelines.
Use the admin path when the request is about tenant settings, governance, licensing, usage metrics, gateways, RLS, or migration governance.
Use the developer path when the request is about embedding, REST APIs, TMSL, custom visuals, or programmatic integration.
Use the DirectQuery path when the request is about live data connectivity, composite models, Direct Lake, or low-latency reporting design.
Use the print-ready reports path when the output must be paginated, print-precise, invoice-style, or export-oriented.
Use the goals and metrics path when the request is really about scorecards and KPI tracking rather than standard report pages or dashboards.
Use the Fabric user path when the request explicitly depends on lakehouses, Fabric pipelines, OneLake, Real-Time Intelligence, or Fabric licensing.
implementation_notes: Treat this directory as a routing aid, not just end-user training. It is useful for deciding whether a Power BI request belongs in Desktop, the service, Report Builder, Fabric, admin tooling, or developer tooling.

When a user says 'build a Power BI dashboard', check whether they mean an interactive report, a service dashboard, a paginated report, a scorecard, or an embedded experience. The learning-path directory separates these effectively.

When building implementation guides or orchestration plans, use the scenario path to name the capability family first, then pull deeper endpoint or modeling knowledge from the corresponding KB entries.
caveats: The directory is a high-level scenario map. It does not replace endpoint-specific API references, modeling documentation, or tenant-governance detail pages.
Some paths deliberately branch into Fabric or adjacent Microsoft Learn areas, so not every linked page is strictly Power BI-only.
Copilot, metrics, and some Fabric-integrated features can have licensing or workspace prerequisites that are not fully captured by the directory itself.
